text: The video displays a 3D reconstruction showing simulations of pancreatic adenocarcinoma and liver metastases in pink. The relative positions of the patient, surgeons, and surgical robot are depicted. An extra-glycemonian approach to the right hilar pedicles is performed, following Takasaki and Sojioka's concepts.
A 3D reconstruction displays simulations of pancreatic adenocarcinoma and liver metastases, with both tumors highlighted in pink.
The image shows the relative positioning of the patient, surgeons, and the surgical robot within the operating room.

The surgeon performs an extra-glycemonian approach to the right hilar pedicles, beginning with a cholecystectomy to identify the posterior section plane of the liver parenchyma. The anterior and posterior right hilar pedicles are identified. Robotic instruments are used to prepare for the Pringle maneuver, utilizing a urinary catheter to apply the Wong loop for bleeding management.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a cholecystectomy, exposing the posterior section plane of the liver parenchyma and identifying the anterior and posterior right hilar pedicles. The Pringle maneuver is prepared using a urinary catheter to manage potential bleeding, facilitated by robotic devices.

The surgeon identifies the right anterior pedicle by separating the lay neck capsule using blunt maneuvers at gates four and five, surrounding the pedicle with a tape. The pedicle is fully encircled and clamped to mark its extension. The right posterior hilar pedicle is similarly identified, surrounded with tape, and clamped, with the ischemia line demarcation becoming visible.

The surgeon identifies the right anterior pedicle by separating the lay neck capsule using blunt maneuvers at gates four and five, surrounding the pedicle with a tape. The pedicle is fully encircled and clamped to mark its extension. The right posterior hilar pedicle is similarly identified and surrounded with tape, followed by clamping. The ischemia line is visibly demarcated.

The surgical robot displays an intraoperative ultrasound image alongside the surgical field. The middle hepatic vein and subsidiary veins of hepatic segments five and eight are identified. The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma using robotic instruments that combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing. Maryland bipolar forceps are used for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. Polymer clips are applied to the vein of segment V, which is clipped and sectioned. The veins of segment eight are identified and sectioned with caution due to the risk of opening. The right anterior and posterior hilar pedicles are sectioned using a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line, employing 3.5 mm staples adjusted to tissue thickness.

The surgical robot displays an intraoperative ultrasound image alongside the surgical field. The middle hepatic vein and subsidiary veins of hepatic segments five and eight are identified. The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma using robotic instruments that combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing. Maryland bipolar forceps are used for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. Polymer clips are applied to the vein of segment V, which is clipped and sectioned. The veins of segment eight are identified and sectioned with caution due to the risk of opening. The right anterior and posterior hilar pedicles are sectioned using a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line, employing 3.5mm staples adjusted to the tissue thickness.

The surgeon dissects along the anterior wall of the vena cava, reaching the entrance of the right hepatic vein. The right hepatic vein is encircled with a tape and sectioned using a robotic endo stapler with 2.5 mm staples. The right liver is then detached from the diaphragm, and small veins draining directly into the vena cava are divided.

The surgeon dissects along the anterior wall of the vena cava, reaching the entrance of the right hepatic vein. The vein is surrounded with a tape and then sectioned using a robotic endo stapler with 2.5 mm staples.

The surgeon frees the right liver from its adhesions to the diaphragm, dividing small veins that drain directly into the vena cava. During the distal pancreatectomy, the inferior part of the pancreas is separated from its adhesions, and the splenic vein and artery are identified and divided using polymer clips and a robotic endo stapler.

The surgeon performs a distal pancreatectomy, freeing the inferior part of the pancreas from adhesions. The splenic vein and artery are identified and divided using polymer clips and a robotic endostapler. The pancreatic parenchyma at the junction of the body and tail is incised with the endostapler. Both surgical specimens are placed in plastic bags for removal.
The surgeon incises the pancreatic parenchyma at the junction of the body and tail using an endostapler. Both surgical specimens are placed into plastic bags for removal.
